coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped carbon dots derived from Sargassum fluitans as fluorophore for DNA detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Godavarthi, S.
Mohan Kumar, K.
Vázquez Vélez, E.
Hernandez-Eligio, A.
Mahendhiran, M.
Hernandez-Como, N.
Aleman, M.
Martinez Gomez, L.
coredata.dc:description: This work focused on the use of waste seaweed Sargassum fluitans (S. fluitans) as carbon source precursor to prepare nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) by hydrothermal method. High resolution transmission electron microscopic (HR-TEM) studies revealed that the synthesized water soluble NCDs are in the size range of 2–8nm and exhibits excellent fluorescent properties with a quantum yield of 18.2%. Elemental nitrogen in NCDs was evidenced by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transformed infrared spectrum (FT-IR). The phytochemical analysis of S. fluitans using 1H NMR and 13C NMR revealed the presence of few amino acids which act as nitrogen source in the preparation of NCDs. Application of NCDs as fluorophore for double stranded DNA, single stranded DNA and RNA detection was highlighted in this study. Excellent fluorescent tagging abilities of NCDs with the biological nucleic acids were evidenced using gel electrophoresis. Significant increase in fluorescence was observed upon tagging of NCDs with nucleic acids and this particular phenomenon helps better in visualizing the nucleic acids. All three nucleic acids i.e. double stranded DNA, single stranded DNA and RNA showed similar phenomenon upon tagging with NCDs. Thus synthesized NCDs may be used as an alternate fluorophore for commercial toxic organic staining agents to visualize nucleic acids.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sargassum fluitans
DNA
RNA and fluorophore

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DNA Agarose Gel Electrophoresis of dsDNA, ssDNA and RNA
Making Double Standard DNA Into Single Standard DNA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images of NCDs at different magnifications.
High resolution XPS spectra of NCDs showing C1s (a), N1s (b) and O1s(c).
PL spectra of NCDs and inset shows normalized PL spectra.
Gel electrophoresis of GSU303 DNA fragment with different tagging agents 1) NCDs 2) EtBr 3) Gel Red and 4) Hydra Green. Along with them, GeneRuler 1Kb (M) DNA molecular marker was added.
Agarose Gel Electrophoresis with different NCDs concentrations 1) 1, 2) 0.5, 3) 0.1, 4) 0.05 and 5) 0.01mg/mL with 50ng of GSU303 DNA in all wells. M: GeneRuler 1Kb DNA molecular marker.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped carbon dots derived from Sargassum fluitans as fluorophore for DNA detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work focused on the use of waste seaweed Sargassum fluitans (S. fluitans) as carbon source precursor to prepare nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) by hydrothermal method. High resolution transmission electron microscopic (HR-TEM) studies revealed that the synthesized water soluble NCDs are in the size range of 2–8nm and exhibits excellent fluorescent properties with a quantum yield of 18.2%. Elemental nitrogen in NCDs was evidenced by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transformed infrared spectrum (FT-IR). The phytochemical analysis of S. fluitans using 1H NMR and 13C NMR revealed the presence of few amino acids which act as nitrogen source in the preparation of NCDs. Application of NCDs as fluorophore for double stranded DNA, single stranded DNA and RNA detection was highlighted in this study. Excellent fluorescent tagging abilities of NCDs with the biological nucleic acids were evidenced using gel electrophoresis. Significant increase in fluorescence was observed upon tagging of NCDs with nucleic acids and this particular phenomenon helps better in visualizing the nucleic acids. All three nucleic acids i.e. double stranded DNA, single stranded DNA and RNA showed similar phenomenon upon tagging with NCDs. Thus synthesized NCDs may be used as an alternate fluorophore for commercial toxic organic staining agents to visualize nucleic acids.

ce:section.ce:para: Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), a genetic carrier with long term storage information is essential for development and proper functioning of biological systems. Fluorescent probes for analyzing DNA have been studied extensively [1]. At present, fluorophores like azo [2], cyanine [3] and imidazole [4] based dyes were being used for detection of biomolecules. The traditional organic tagging agents have certain limitations like low solubility rates, low photo-stability, high toxicity and solubility in aqueous medium [5]. Materials which overcome the above limitations are essential to use in biological research. Based on fluorescence response, few semiconductor nanomaterials were employed to detect biological specimens which showed good sensitivity and selectivity [6]. Material like CdTe quantum dots-cationic phorphyrins nanohybrids is also reported as sensor for DNA [7]. But the metal based semiconductor quantum dots are having issues like toxicity and bio-compatibility when applied in real time in-vivo biological applications [8]. In addition, the expensive nature of the semiconductors hinders their specific use in commercial applications.

So, Carbon quantum dots (CDs) with properties similar to that of semiconductor might serve to overcome all the above said difficulties. CDs are newly classified member in carbon family with size less 10nm possessing unique properties [9]. When compared to the traditional organic dyes and semiconductors quantum dots, CDs exhibit good photo-stability, low toxicity and high solubility rates in aqueous media [9]. Resemblance of semiconductor properties and low toxicity owed CDs as a promising candidate for biological applications such as in vivo and in vitro optical imaging, drug carriers, photodynamic therapy (PDT) and chemotherapy [10]. On the other hand, heteroatom doping into the carbon nanostructures enormously increase its optical properties [11]. The properties of the CDs depend on the method of preparations and can be done via thermal oxidation, laser ablation, microwave assisted passivation, pyrolysis, electrochemical exfoliation and hydrothermal treatment etc. [9]. Among the synthetic methods, hydrothermal method is more preferable due to its simplicity and cost effectiveness [9]. Surface passivation with hydroxyl and carboxylic groups results in enhanced photoluminescence and water solubility nature [12]. Selection of precursor materials for CDs preparation depends on the type of method being adopted for synthesis. Generally used carbon precursors are graphitic oxide, carbon soot and activated carbon [13]. In recent years molecular precursors like citric acid [14], glucose [15] and various amino acids [16] were being used to prepare CDs, not only to avoid toxicity induced from the by-products but also to avoid complex post treatment for passivating the CDs. In order to avoid expensive precursors, naturally available green sources of carbon like various vegetables [17], willow bark [18], milk [19], biomass [20], fruit juices [21] and fruit peels [22] have been used to produce CDs and hetero atom (N or S) doped CDs.

Most common method to separate and identify DNA is gel electrophoresis and it requires fluorescent dye in visualizing DNA fragments [23]. Among the commercial available nucleic acid stains, Ethidium bromide (EtBr) is commonly used fluorophore, in spite of its carcinogenetic nature. Even though, it is economical and highly stable, the disposal costs are very high as necessary steps should be taken to avoid the contamination of the environment. In the present work, Sargassum fluitans (S. fluitans) a sea weed found abundantly in the Caribbean Sea with rich amino acids and carbohydrates [24] was selected as green precursor to produce nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs). Even though reports says that it possess excellent therapeutic activities as they produces metabolites of structural classes like terpenoids, steroids, glycerides and polysaccharides etc. [24], free floating S. fluitans is a big problem for tourists and beach officials. The present work focuses on, (i) synthesizing NCDs using S. fluitans which is rich in elemental carbon and nitrogen as green source. (ii) Characterizing the obtained NCDs by all means and (iii) Applying developed fluorescent NCDs as fluorophore to detect DNA and comparing its efficiency with some traditional organic fluorophores using gel electrophoresis. To the best of our knowledge there is no reports for synthesizing heteroatom doped CDs using seaweeds.

S. fluitans sea weed was collected from the beach sides in Progreso, a port city in the Yucatán state, located in Gulf of Mexico. 25g of collected sea weed was placed in Erlenmeyer flask and stirred with 2L of distilled water. After 30min, the water was removed and fresh water was introduced once again into the flask and stirred. This process of washing was done for 6–8 times in order to removal salts and other water soluble impurities. The washed sea weed was then dried well and pulverized. 10g of powdered sea weed was mixed with 100mL of distilled water and refluxed it 30min. After cooling the content in flask, extract was filtered using Whatman No.1 filter paper and stored it in a refrigerator for further use.

10mL of prepared extract was diluted to 50mL using distilled water. This diluted extract was introduced into a 100mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at 180°C in furnace for 5h. The content in the autoclave was allowed to cool down naturally. The obtained pale yellow solution was centrifuged at 10000rpm and the supernatant was filtered using a cellulose nitrate membrane (0.2μm) and dialyzed. The obtained pale yellow solution was further subjected to characterization.

UV–Vis spectrum of NCDs was recorded using HITACHI U-5100 UV–Visible double beam spectrophotometer. FT-IR spectrum was obtained using Varian 660-IR FT-IR spectrophotometer in order to get information regarding surface functionalization of NCDs. The size and morphology of the synthesized NCDs were determined by High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM) JEOL JEM 2100. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of the NCDs sample was measured with ThermoFisher-VG instrument equipped with a nonmonochromatic Al Kα (1486.7eV) X-ray source and a hemispherical electron analyzer with seven channeltrons (model XPS110).

DNA fragments corresponding to GSU303 region were amplified from Geobacter sulfurreducens chromosomal DNA by PCR using primers: IG_303F and IG_303R [25]. The PCR product was obtained with an amplicon size of 146bp. The PCR mixture (25μL) contained dNTP mix (0.2mM each, Thermo Scientific, USA, 0.5μM each specific primer, ~20ng of DNA, 1× PCR buffer (Thermo Scientific, USA), 1.5mM MgCl2 (Thermo Scientific, USA), and 5U Taq-DNA polymerase (Thermo Scientific, USA). The PCR reaction was performed in an Eppendorf Master Cycler (Life Sciences Biotech, USA) with initial denaturation at 94°C for 3min, followed by 30cycles of 94°C for 40s, with an annealing temperature at 60°C for 1min and final extension temperature at 72°C for 15s. The DNA concentration was determined by using Nanodrop 2000c, Thermo Scientific, USA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA Agarose Gel Electrophoresis of dsDNA, ssDNA and RNA
ce:section.ce:para: Double Stranded DNA and Single stranded DNA (50ng) and RNA (100ng) was loaded on a 1% (w/v) agarose gel with 1× Tris Borate EDTA (TBE) buffer; 0.1μg/μL of Gene Ruler 1Kb DNA ladder (Thermo Scientific, USA) was included as a size-standard marker. GSU303 DNA fragment with different staining agents along with fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) was subjected to gel electrophoresis performed at 120V for 45min and the DNA bands were detected by visualizing under UV light (254nm wavelength) in Gel Doc EZ Imager (BIO RAD Gel Documentation System, California, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Making Double Standard DNA Into Single Standard DNA
ce:section.ce:para: The GSU303 DNA fragment (200ng/μL) was heated at 95°C, for 10min by using thermoblack BIORAD T100™ Thermal Cycler (California, USA) and immediately kept into ice in order to maintain a single strand DNA (to avoid re-annealing). This single stranded DNA with carbon dots was tested in Gel electrophoresis and compared with that of EtBr (positive control).

Total RNA from Geobacter sulfurreducens was obtained using RNeasy Mini Kit according to the instructions of the supplier (Qiagen, Germany). RNA (100ng) was mixed with carbon dots and run in agarose 1% electrophoresis with TBE 1× buffer. The electrophoresis was performed at 100V for 45min and the RNA bands were detected visualized using a BIO RAD Gel Doc™ EZ Imager (Gel Documentation System, California, USA).
DNA concentration was 50ng for all the samples. For all intercalating dyes, same concentration and same volume of DNA was added in order to compare the intensity of bands.

S. fluitans sea weed was chosen as source to produce NCDs due to its carbon and nitrogen elemental rich nature. On the other hand, in recent years floating sea weeds were making their way to shores in uncontrolled manner and endangering the tourism of Mexico. In spite of its little ecological benefits, the abundant availability and hetero elemental rich nature of the seaweed can be a greener option as source of carbon and nitrogen to produce NCDs. The phytochemical analysis of S. fluitans using 1H NMR and 13C NMR revealed the presence of water soluble amino acids like alanine, glutamic acid etc. (Figs. S1–S4). The complete phytochemical analysis was given in the supplementary information. These amino acids act as nitrogen source in the preparation of NCDs. One step hydrothermal treatment of S. fluitans extract results in pale yellowish solution which confirms the successful carbonization [16,17]. HR-TEM images of NCDs were shown in (Fig. 1 ) which clearly indicates that the NCDs were well dispersed and roughly spherical in shape with size in the range of 2–8nm. A little increase in size of NCDs was due to that prolonged reaction time favours the doping of nitrogen into carbon frame work and this phenomenon results in the increased size of NCDs.

The passivation and elemental composition was studied using ATR-FTIR and XPS measurements. ATR-FTIR spectra of NCDs were shown in Fig. 2 . The band at 1058cm−1 can be assigned to COC stretching vibrations. The bands at 3286cm−1 and 3444cm−1 corresponds to OH stretching and band at 3567cm−1 corresponds to NH stretching. The peaks at 1234, 1396 and 1459cm−1 corresponds to CO stretching, COH bending and CN stretching, respectively. NH bending can be observed at 1513 and 1556cm−1, NH2 scissoring at 1649cm−1. The peaks at 2901 and 2980cm−1 corresponds to CH stretching and CO peaks resembled at 1695 and 1742cm−1. It is clear from FTIR results that synthesized NCDs' surface is passivated with biomolecules.

Further, XPS measurements were conducted to confirm the surface functionalization and elemental composition of the prepared NCDs. Three distinct peaks at 284.1, 400.5 and 532.1eV are ascribed to C1s, N1s and O1s respectively. C and O are dominating elements in NCDs due to the formation of graphitic carbon and oxygen rich functional groups as revealed earlier by FTIR. The nitrogen content in the NCDs was found to be 9.85% which indicates the successful doping of elemental nitrogen into carbon dots. The high resolution XPS of C1s region was shown in Fig. 3a and has three distinct peaks at 283.2, 284.3 and 288.2eV indicates three different types of carbon bonds (CC, CO and CO/CN, respectively). The high resolution of N1s region shows a broad spectrum with three peaks at 398.9, 400.5 and 402.2eV, as shown in Fig. 3b, corresponds to presence of CN, CN and ONC bonds respectively. Also, the broad region O1s is deconvoluted into three peaks at 529.9, 531.0, 532.05eV and assigned to CO/NO, CO/NO and CO/NO respectively. FTIR and XPS results confirm the doping of elemental nitrogen and functionalization of NCDs with carboxylic, carbonyl, phenolic and amine groups.

UV–Vis absorption spectrum was given in Fig. S5 of supplementary data and the inset shows the visual variation of NCD's under visible light and in presence of UV light (365nm). The absorption peak at 226nm can be ascribed to n-π* transition from core section of carbon [26]. The absorption band around 280nm was due to π-π* transition from the surface/molecular section [26]. Fig. 4 shows the PL spectra of NCDs at different excitation wavelength in water. A red shift in the emission spectra with simultaneous decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed with increasing wavelengths from 340nm to 400nm with an increment of 20nm. The strongest emission band is positioned at 450nm for excitation wavelength of 340nm. These PL observations look similar to that of previous reports [27]. No much difference in the PL characteristic of NCDs in phosphate buffer (pH=7.2) was observed. Further quantum yield of 18.2% was observed for NCDs using quinine sulphate (in 0.1M H2SO4 with quantum yield 54% at 360nm) as reference. So, the observed optical characteristic of the NCDs owed its use in biological detection as an effective fluorescent probe.

Agarose Gel Electrophoresis is a well-known molecular biology technique which helps to discrete the biomolecules by their size, in which the smaller sized ones move distant than the larger ones. DNA is negatively charged molecule and it moves towards the positive in the electrophoresis buffer [28]. In molecular biology, fluorescent tags are very important in order to visualize nucleic acids such as DNA and RNA fragments under UV transilluminator. EtBr was commonly used fluorophore with planer structure that can intercalate DNA, thereby results in increased fluorescence intensity which in turns helps to visualize DNA. But EtBr is a potent mutagen and well known for its carcinogenity.

Tagging ability of NCDs was evaluated using gel electrophoresis with GSU303 DNA and compared with different commercial tagging agents like gel red, hydra green, EtBr (Fig. 5 ). Among the tested tagging agents, EtBr and NCDs shows excellent tagging capabilities towards GSU303 DNA and those bands are visualized clearly under UV-illuminator. This particular observation indicated that NCDs were equally capable like EtBr to visualize DNA. Gel red and hydra green does not show fluorescence because, these dyes need to be stained after running the gel electrophoresis [29]. And also, the engineered structures of these two dyes are incompetent to cross the cell membranes. Though NCDs interacts in similar manner as EtBr interacts with DNA and RNA, they may not act as frame shift mutants due to its natural origination and non-toxic nature [16].

On the other hand, the fluorescent agents EtBr, Gel Red and Hydra green along with NCDs were directly added to 50mL of running buffer (TBE buffer) 1× concentration, stained after agarose gel electrophoresis and viewed under UV transilluminator in order to visualize the DNA fragments (Fig. S6a–d of Supplementary data). In this method of staining, we found that all the tested dyes along with NCDs showed excellent band of tested DNA as shown in Fig. S6(a–d). So, NCDs can be used either for in-gel or after gel electrophoresis in order to visualize the DNA bands.

Fig. S7(a) showed that NCDs without DNA shows no fluorescence, but NCDs tagged with DNA shows an excellent florescent band. This phenomenon was due to the fact that fluorescence intensity of NCDs increases predominantly in the presence of DNA [30]. This observation clearly indicates that NCDs intercalate DNA molecule. The increased fluorescence of NCD-DNA mixture was assumed to be resulted from the interaction of passivated functional groups of NCDs with DNA bases. The increased fluorescence upon interaction of NCDs with DNA can be assumed that lone pairs of electron from oxygen and nitrogen in passivated NCDs bounds with the bases of DNA by means of hydrogen bonds which in turn help in visualizing the DNA.

Further we investigated the interaction of Single stranded DNA and RNA with NCDs. Fig. S7(b) of Supplementary data shows that EtBr and NCD's (both 10μg/μL) exhibits similar fluorescence band when tagged with ssDNA and visualized under UV transilluminator. Apart from dsDNA and ssDNA visualization (Fig. S7(a) and (b) of Supplementary data), total RNA (50ng/μL) was also mixed with NCDs directly (Fig. S7(c) of Supplementary data), but it was unable to stain after electrophoresis because unlike DNA, RNA is highly unstable nature. So, only in-gel electrophoresis alone was performed for single stranded RNA tagged with NCDs. In order to find optimum concentration of NCDs required to visualize double stranded DNA, in-gel electrophoresis was performed with five different concentrations of NCDs (1, 0.5, 0.1, 0.05 and 0.01mg/mL) tagged in 30ng of double stranded DNA in all wells. From Fig. 6 , it is clear that all tested concentrations of NCDs show clear band with double stranded DNA From this observation it is clear that even 0.01mg/mL is sufficient for proper visualization of DNA.

NCDs with good quantum yield were synthesized successfully using S. fluitans as carbon source. XPS studies confirm the presence of elemental nitrogen in carbon dots. Further, surface passivation of NCDs was confirmed by XPS and FTIR. HR-TEM reveals that NCDs were well dispersed and falls in the size range of 2–8nm. The synthesized NCDs acted as excellent fluorophores to detect DNA bands in gel electrophoresis. We demonstrated the use of NCDs as tagging agent for nucleic acid (dsDNA, ssDNA and RNA) in both in-gel and after-gel electrophoresis. Converting the huge waste and abundantly available S. flutians into very useful non-toxic NCDs with potential tagging abilities may be an alternative to commercially available toxic dyes like EtBr to visualize nucleic acids. A deeper insight to bring exact mechanism and interaction pathways with DNA, changes in confirmation and destabilizations are under progress in authors' laboratories.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Dra. Maura Casales Diaz, ICF, UNAM for her assistance and support. S. Godavarthi, K. Mohan Kumar and M. Muhilan were thankful to the postdoctoral scholarship from Direccion General de Asuntos del Personal Academico - Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico (DGAPA-UNAM). We thank Dr. V. Agarwal of CIICAp-UAEM for providing PL spectral analysis. We appreciate the help of Carlos Narvaez Zugasti in collecting Sargassum fluitans.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2017.05.014.
